Entity_Description: Gold River is a village municipality in the Strathcona Regional District on the west coast of Vancouver Island, British Columbia, near the head of Muchalat Inlet where the Gold and Heber Rivers meet. Built in 1965 as a planned company town for the Tahsis Company (later Bowater) pulp mill, which closed in 1998, it recorded a population of 1,212 in the 2021 Census. The village marks the western terminus of Highway 28 and serves as the mainland gateway to Nootka Sound and Strathcona Provincial Park.

Call {PHONE}.
Short_Description: Purpose-built in 1965 to house workers at the Tahsis Company pulp mill, Gold River rose as one of British Columbia's fully planned towns, tucked at the head of Muchalat Inlet where the Gold and Heber Rivers converge. When the mill closed in 1998, the village reinvented itself around kayaking, caving and the MV Uchuck III sailings to Nootka Sound. That compact, mid-1960s townsite and its damp coastal setting shape exactly what {SERVICE} local properties need. {COMPANY_NAME} delivers {SERVICE} built for Gold River's homes and businesses, backing every job with {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP}. Whether you are near the village core or out along Highway 28, we bring {BUSINESS_TYPE} expertise that respects this remote coastal community. Ready to get started? Call {PHONE}.
Long_Description:
Few Vancouver Island communities have a beginning as deliberate as Gold River's. In 1965 the townsite was laid out from scratch on the plateau above Muchalat Inlet to serve the new Tahsis Company pulp mill, giving the village a tidy, uniform stock of mid-1960s houses along curving residential streets — a building profile that still shapes the {SERVICE} demands here. When the Bowater mill shut its gates in 1998, Gold River pivoted from single-industry mill town to an outdoor-recreation gateway, and its properties now range from original townsite homes to riverside and view lots.

Geography defines daily life. The village sits where the Gold and Heber Rivers meet before draining into Muchalat Inlet, hemmed in by the peaks of the Vancouver Island Ranges and the edge of Strathcona Provincial Park — British Columbia's oldest — just to the north. Highway 28 dead-ends here after crossing the island from Campbell River, roughly an hour and a half east, which means {COMPANY_NAME} plans routes and scheduling around a genuinely remote coastal address rather than a suburban grid.

The climate is emphatically wet. Storms funnel up the inlet and rain pours off the surrounding ranges through the long winter, testing roofs, drainage, exteriors and anything exposed to standing moisture. {COMPANY_NAME} tailors {SERVICE} to that reality, because {SERVICE_DESCRIPTION} that ignores Gold River's rainfall and marine air simply doesn't last.

The village is also the mainland launch point for Nootka Sound: the freight-and-passenger vessel MV Uchuck III sails from the dock to Yuquot and remote coastal camps, while kayakers, anglers and cavers bound for the nearby Upana Caves stage through town. That seasonal, tourism-driven rhythm creates its own service patterns for the {BUSINESS_TYPE} trades.

For nearby coverage, {COMPANY_NAME} also reaches communities across the Strathcona Regional District, including Campbell River and Quadra Island. From the townsite core to the docks at the inlet, we bring {SERVICE} that reflects what Gold River residents expect — {TAGLINE} Call {PHONE} or email {EMAIL} to book your {ESTIMATE_TYPE}.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 1,212 (2021 Census)
- Founded in 1965 as a planned company town for the Tahsis Company pulp mill, which closed in 1998
- Located at the head of Muchalat Inlet, where the Gold and Heber Rivers meet
- Marks the western terminus of Highway 28, the cross-island route from Campbell River
- Mainland departure point for the MV Uchuck III to Nootka Sound and Yuquot (Friendly Cove)
- Gateway to Strathcona Provincial Park and the nearby Upana Caves
- Wet coastal temperate-rainforest climate with heavy winter rainfall; part of the Strathcona Regional District
